does: This paper introduces Eval-Skill, a method for improving reward modeling in open-ended domains by synthesizing reusable evaluation skills that judges can apply across multiple queries. Rather than generating rubrics per-query or training reward model parameters, Eval-Skill uses only 100 cases per domain to progressively generate evaluation skills through two stages: workflow generation (how to approach evaluation) followed by principle generation (what criteria matter). The method employs exploration and selection mechanisms across both stages to discover effective evaluation approaches, then injects these skills directly into the judge's context during inference. The approach demonstrates consistent improvements across RewardBench benchmarks, with substantial gains ranging from 13.44% for smaller models like Qwen3-8B and higher improvements for larger backbones.
connects: This work builds on recent efforts to improve reward modeling through better guidance mechanisms, extending beyond static rubric-based approaches that have proven inflexible for nuanced preference judgments. It responds to limitations in per-query rubric generation methods, which add inference overhead and often fail to capture domain-specific subtleties consistently. The paper connects to broader lines of work on in-context learning and skill discovery in language models, drawing on techniques for synthesizing reusable patterns rather than parameter-level adaptation. It also relates to prior research on exploration strategies for discovering effective prompting patterns and the growing recognition that context injection can be more efficient than full model fine-tuning for specialized tasks.
enables: This approach opens pathways for more efficient reward modeling systems that require minimal labeled data per domain while maintaining high quality judgments at inference time. The emphasis on reusable skills could enable reward models to scale across diverse domains without repeated expensive optimization steps, making human-AI preference alignment more practical. The two-stage skill synthesis pipeline suggests future work on discovering and composing evaluation skills hierarchically or across related domains, potentially enabling transfer learning for judgment tasks. The method also points toward developing frameworks for automatically auditing and improving evaluation skills, understanding what makes certain judgment principles robust, and extending skill discovery to other structured reasoning tasks beyond reward modeling.
